sections.en_html:
• Etymology: The compound joins ethics and negotiation. Ethics derives from the Greek ethos (ἤθος) meaning "character, custom, habitual dwelling", mediated through the Latin ethica (Aristotle, Nicomachean Ethics, c. 350 BCE; MacIntyre, 1981). Negotiation descends from the Latin negotium (nec + otium, literally "not-leisure", i.e. business), via negotiari "to carry on business" (Lewicki, Saunders & Barry, 2020).
• Literal meaning: Thus the field literally denotes "the principles of right character governing the conduct of business bargaining" — the normative evaluation of conduct, claims, and tactics within a bargaining encounter (Reitz, Wall & Love, 1998).
• Arabic translation: The most precise rendering is أخلاقيات التفاوض (akhlāqiyyāt al-tafāwuḍ). Al-tafāwuḍ (Form VI of f-w-ḍ, "to delegate/empower mutually") captures the reciprocal authority-granting of bargaining better than musāwama (haggling over price) or mufāwaḍa (formal diplomatic talks). Akhlāq is the canonical Arabic term for the science of disposition and virtue (al-Ghazālī, Iḥyāʾ ʿUlūm al-Dīn, c. 1105).
• Historical names: Among Western scholars it appeared as "the morality of bargaining" (Carr, 1968) and "negotiation morality" before stabilising as "negotiation ethics" (Lewicki & Robinson, 1998). In the Islamic juristic tradition the cognate concerns fall under أدب البيع (ādāb al-bayʿ, the etiquette of sale) and فقه المعاملات (fiqh al-muʿāmalāt).

• Showcase: Negotiation ethics is the systematic study of which tactics, claims, omissions, and behaviours are morally permissible when parties bargain over interests under conditions of conflicting goals and asymmetric information (Lewicki, Saunders & Barry, 2020). Its paradigm case is the bluff: a negotiator privately willing to accept $80,000 who announces "I cannot go below $95,000" — is this a permissible tactic or a lie? (Carr, 1968; Strudler, 1995).
• Strict boundary (al-ḥadd): The genus is applied normative ethics; the differentia is restriction to the dyadic/multiparty bargaining encounter and its instrumental communication. It evaluates conduct qua tactic deployed to influence a counterpart's perception of the bargaining range, not conduct in general (Reitz, Wall & Love, 1998).
• Disambiguation (1) — Business ethics: The broader parent field governs all commercial conduct (corporate governance, marketing, labour); negotiation ethics is the species concerned specifically with the bargaining table (Bowie, 1999).
(2) Negotiation tactics / strategy: a descriptive, instrumental discipline asking "what works?"; negotiation ethics is normative, asking "what is permissible?" (Fisher & Ury, 1981).
(3) Bargaining theory (game theory): a formal-mathematical model of rational concession (Nash, 1950); ethics supplies the moral constraints that game theory brackets out.

• The exact phenomenon: The subject matter (mawḍūʿstrategic communication under conflicting interests — specifically the gap between what a negotiator knows/intends and what the negotiator discloses (Lewicki, Saunders & Barry, 2020).
• Scope: It studies (i) truth and deception — lies, half-truths, omissions, and puffery about reservation prices, alternatives (BATNA), and authority; (ii) information asymmetry — duties to disclose latent defects versus the rule of caveat emptor (Strudler, 1995); (iii) tactical pressure — threats, deadlines, and emotional manipulation; and (iv) fairness of outcome and process (Fisher & Ury, 1981).
• Constitutive tension: Its defining problem is the "negotiator's dilemma" (Lax & Sebenius, 1986): the same information-withholding that creates competitive advantage (claiming value) corrodes the trust required for cooperative gains (creating value). The discipline exists precisely because partial concealment is structurally intrinsic to bargaining (Reitz, Wall & Love, 1998).

• Formalisation: Negotiation ethics has no single "founder"; its modern crystallisation is conventionally traced to Albert Carr's provocation (Carr, 1968), which framed bargaining as a "game" with its own permissive morality (the "poker analogy"), forcing the field into existence by scandalising business ethicists.
• Dated timeline:
(1) c. 350 BCE — Aristotle grounds virtue and truthfulness in the Nicomachean Ethics, the deep root of all later inquiry.
(2) 1968 — Carr, "Is Business Bluffing Ethical?" (Harvard Business Review), the catalysing provocation.
(3) 1981 — Fisher & Ury, Getting to Yes, embedding fairness and "principled negotiation" in practice.
(4) 1986 — Lax & Sebenius articulate the "negotiator's dilemma", the field's structural core.
(5) 1995–1998 — Strudler (1995) on the morality of bluffing; Lewicki & Robinson (1998) supply the first empirical taxonomy of ethically marginal tactics.

• Category: Negotiation ethics is a sub-branch of applied normative ethics, itself a division of practical philosophy (Bowie, 1999). By disciplinary home it is a hybrid of business ethics, organisational behaviour, and conflict-resolution studies (Lewicki, Saunders & Barry, 2020).
• Subcategory: Within it lie (i) the ethics of deception/disclosure, (ii) the ethics of distributive (claiming) versus integrative (creating) bargaining, and (iii) cross-cultural negotiation ethics (Reitz, Wall & Love, 1998).
• Its standing (nisbah): It is subaltern to general ethical theory (drawing principles from utilitarianism, deontology, contractarianism, virtue ethics) yet autonomous in object, because its bargaining context generates problems — the permissibility of bluffing, the duty to correct a counterpart's mistaken belief — absent from general ethics (Strudler, 1995).
• Neighbouring relations: It is sister to marketing ethics (advertising claims), legal ethics (lawyer-as-negotiator duties), and game theory (its descriptive twin), and is the parent genus of the meta-entry "ethics typology synthesis" that systematises its classifications.

• Epistemological roots: Negotiation ethics derives its axioms from the four canonical moral traditions (Lewicki, Saunders & Barry, 2020): (i) consequentialism/utilitarianism — act so as to maximise aggregate welfare (Bentham, 1789; Mill, 1863); (ii) deontology — the categorical imperative forbidding treating others merely as means, grounding the duty not to deceive (Kant, 1785).
• Continued: (iii) social-contract theory — principles all rational parties would accept behind a veil of ignorance (Rawls, 1971); (iv) virtue ethics — conduct expressing a truthful, just character (Aristotle, c. 350 BCE; MacIntyre, 1981).
• Seminal methodological texts: It draws operational method from the behavioural science of bargaining (Walton & McKersie, 1965) and from the principled-negotiation framework (Fisher & Ury, 1981), while the bluffing problematic is sharpened by analytic philosophy (Carr, 1968; Strudler, 1995).
• Islamic derivation: In the Islamic stream, axioms derive directly from primary text — the Qur'anic command to fulfil contracts (Q 5:1) and weigh with justice (Q 17:35), and the Prophetic prohibition of ghishsh (deception) and gharar (excessive uncertainty) in transactions (al-Bukhārī; Muslim).

• Fundamental questions: The discipline unfolds through a logical sequence of problems (Lewicki, Saunders & Barry, 2020):
(1) Is bargaining a special-ethics "game"? Carr's poker analogy versus the objection that business has non-consenting third parties (Carr, 1968; Bowie, 1999).
(2) What counts as a lie versus permissible non-disclosure? The distinction between active misrepresentation and silence (Strudler, 1995).
(3) Is bluffing about reservation price deception? The classic case dividing the field (Carr, 1968; Strudler, 1995).
(4) When is there a duty to disclose? Latent defects, counterpart error, and the limits of caveat emptor
(5) Are pressure tactics (threats, false deadlines, good-cop/bad-cop) coercive? (Reitz, Wall & Love, 1998).
• Branches: From these grow (i) the theory of deceptive tactics, (ii) the ethics of information exchange, (iii) procedural-fairness theory, and (iv) cross-cultural and agency (lawyer/broker) negotiation ethics.

• The fruit (al-thamarah): The tangible benefit is a practitioner able to claim legitimate value without forfeiting integrity, sustain long-term trust-based relationships, avoid legal liability for fraud and misrepresentation, and convert ethically marginal pressure into durable, reputation-preserving agreements (Lewicki, Saunders & Barry, 2020; Fisher & Ury, 1981).
• Instrumental value: Empirically, ethical reputation functions as bargaining capital: counterparts concede more and police less when they trust the negotiator, so principled conduct is also strategically superior in repeated dealings (Lewicki & Robinson, 1998).
• Intrinsic merit (al-faḍl): Its rank is high because it governs ḥuqūq al-ʿibādḥifẓ al-mālal-Muwāfaqāt

• Ruling on study (al-ḥukm): Mastering negotiation ethics is, for the community, a farḍ kifāyahmaṣlaḥah ʿāmmahfarḍ ʿaynal-Muwāfaqāt
• Threshold of fulfilment: The farḍ kifāyahghararghishsh
• Primary-text grounding: The axis is the Qur'anic command "O you who believe, fulfil [your] contracts" (Q 5:1) and "do not consume one another's wealth unjustly... unless by mutual consent" (Q 4:29), with the Prophetic rule "whoever deceives is not of us" (Muslim) and the prohibition of bayʿ al-ghararḥarām
• Maqasid prioritisation: Under Maqasid al-Shari'ah, preserving wealth and trust (ḥifẓ al-māllaḍ arar wa-lā ḍirār

• (1) Aristotle, Nicomachean Ethics (c. 350 BCE): The foundational treatise on virtue and truthfulness; the deep root of all later negotiation-ethics inquiry.
(2) Immanuel Kant, Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals (1785): Establishes the deontological prohibition on deception and treating persons as mere means.
(3) Albert Z. Carr, "Is Business Bluffing Ethical?" Harvard Business Review (1968): The catalysing "poker analogy" article that founded the modern debate.
(4) Roger Fisher & William Ury, Getting to Yes (1981): The canonical statement of principled, interest-based negotiation embedding fairness norms.
(5) Roy J. Lewicki, David M. Saunders & Bruce Barry, Negotiation (8th ed., 2020): The standard graduate text systematising the field, including the empirical taxonomy of ethically marginal tactics.
